value:
# Support `cycle://` Protocol Links Specification

Status: Draft
Ticket: `UKN-B9NZJ`
Conformance target: `@cycle/desktop`

## 1. Normative Language

The key words `MUST`, `MUST NOT`, `REQUIRED`, `SHOULD`, `SHOULD NOT`, `RECOMMENDED`, `MAY`, and `OPTIONAL` in this specification are to be interpreted as described in RFC 2119 and RFC 8174 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals.

`Implementation-defined` means the implementation may choose the exact mechanism, UI presentation, or platform detail, but the choice MUST be documented in code, tests, or user-facing behavior and MUST preserve the requirements in this ticket.

## 2. Purpose

Cycle Desktop MUST provide first-class support for `cycle://` links so Cycle references can be copied, rendered, clicked, shared externally, opened by the operating system, and routed inside the app without falling back to arbitrary browser navigation.

The v1 conformance target MUST support repository and ticket links. Additional targets such as rooms, boards, saved views, or project memory references MAY be added only when they have explicit URI grammar, route mapping, failure behavior, and tests equivalent to the v1 targets.

## 3. Problem Statement

Cycle currently needs a stable deep-link contract for repository and ticket references. Links appear in chat, markdown, issue bodies, comments, Slack, GitHub, browser pages, documents, and terminal output. Internal clicks MUST navigate within Cycle. External clicks MUST open or focus the Cycle desktop app and route to the referenced object when possible.

A conforming implementation MUST avoid these failure modes:

- Treating `cycle://` links as normal web URLs.
- Passing `cycle://` links through `shell.openExternal` from inside the renderer.
- Losing protocol links received during cold start.
- Opening duplicate usable app sessions for an external link.
- Navigating to malformed, unsupported, or unsafe targets.
- Failing silently when a target is missing, inaccessible, deleted, or unsupported.

## 4. Goals

- Define a canonical `cycle://` URI grammar for repository and ticket targets.
- Add a shared parser and serializer that can be used by main-process, preload, renderer, and tests.
- Route internal `cycle://` clicks through the existing workspace navigation layer.
- Register Cycle Desktop as the OS handler for external `cycle://` links on supported desktop platforms.
- Focus an existing app instance when possible and route the external link exactly once.
- Queue external protocol events until the main window and renderer navigation layer are ready.
- Provide clear user-visible fallback behavior for invalid or unresolved links.
- Preserve the existing security boundary around external URL opening.

## 5. Non-Goals

- This issue MUST NOT define a general-purpose custom protocol browser, network fetcher, file opener, or command execution surface.
- This issue MUST NOT relax the existing `openExternal` allowlist for renderer-initiated external URLs.
- This issue MUST NOT require background mutation, remote sync, ticket creation, repository registration, or destructive recovery just because a link was opened.
- This issue MUST NOT require web builds to become the OS-level handler for `cycle://` links.
- This issue MUST NOT require support for rooms, boards, views, project memory, workspace/account IDs, or cross-account routing in v1 unless the implementation extends this specification with equivalent tests.

## 6. URI Contract

### 6.1 Required v1 URI Forms

The implementation MUST support these canonical forms:

- `cycle://repository/<repositoryId>`
- `cycle://repository/<repositoryId>/tickets/<ticketId>`

The implementation MUST map canonical v1 targets as follows:

| URI | Parsed target | Workspace route |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cycle://repository/<repositoryId>` | repository target | `/repositories/:repositoryId/issues` |
| `cycle://repository/<repositoryId>/tickets/<ticketId>` | ticket target | `/repositories/:repositoryId/issues/:ticketId` |

The renderer SHOULD generate workspace routes through the existing route helper layer, such as `toWorkspacePath`, rather than hand-building route strings in unrelated components.

### 6.2 Parsing Rules

A conforming parser MUST:

- Accept only the `cycle:` scheme.
- Treat target keywords such as `repository` and `tickets` as case-sensitive lowercase literals.
- Reject empty segments.
- Reject query strings and fragments for v1.
- Reject username, password, port, and opaque URL forms.
- Percent-decode each identifier segment exactly once.
- Reject decoded identifier segments containing `/`, `\\`, NUL, ASCII control characters, or more than 256 Unicode code points.
- Reject unsupported target kinds instead of partially interpreting them.
- Return a typed target union equivalent to:
  - `{ kind: "repository", repositoryId }`
  - `{ kind: "ticket", repositoryId, ticketId }`

A conforming serializer MUST emit canonical lowercase target keywords and MUST percent-encode identifier segments with URI component encoding.

## 7. Component Requirements

### 7.1 Shared Protocol Module

The implementation MUST add a shared protocol module that has no dependency on Electron, React, DOM APIs, local storage, HTTP clients, or shell APIs. It MUST expose at least:

- `parseCycleProtocolUrl(input): CycleProtocolParseResult`
- `toCycleProtocolUrl(target): string`
- `cycleProtocolTargetToWorkspaceLocation(target): WorkspaceLocation | error`

The parse result MUST distinguish malformed URLs from unsupported-but-well-formed targets so UI and logs can present accurate failure reasons.

### 7.2 Internal Link Handling

Renderer surfaces that render markdown or rich links MUST intercept primary activation of `cycle://` links and route them through the in-app protocol handler.

Internal handling MUST:

- Call `preventDefault()` before any browser or Electron external navigation occurs.
- Use the same shared parser as external protocol handling.
- Navigate repository and ticket targets through the workspace router.
- Preserve existing behavior for `http:`, `https:`, `mailto:`, and other already-supported non-Cycle links.
- Show a user-visible error or empty state for malformed, unsupported, missing, unauthorized, or deleted targets.

Internal handling MUST NOT call `window.cycleDesktop.openExternal()` for `cycle://` URLs.

### 7.3 Main-Process Protocol Registration

Cycle Desktop MUST register itself as the handler for the `cycle` protocol where the host operating system supports custom URL schemes.

The main process MUST handle these platform paths:

- macOS `open-url` events.
- Windows and Linux initial process arguments for cold-start protocol opens.
- Windows and Linux second-instance arguments when Cycle is already running.

The implementation MUST use Electron single-instance behavior so an external `cycle://` open focuses the existing usable app instance instead of creating a duplicate usable session.

Protocol registration in development mode MAY be implementation-defined, but packaged application behavior MUST be documented and testable by manual QA.

### 7.4 Protocol Event Queue and Renderer Readiness

External protocol events MUST be represented as immutable protocol events with a stable event id, raw URL, received timestamp, source platform path, parsed target or parse error, and delivery state.

The main process MUST queue valid external protocol events until all of the following are true:

- Electron is ready.
- The main window exists and is not destroyed.
- The renderer has loaded.
- The renderer has explicitly signaled that the workspace router can accept protocol navigation.

The renderer MUST expose a readiness signal over the preload/IPC boundary. The main process MUST drain queued events in FIFO order after readiness. A queued event MUST be dispatched no more than once unless the renderer explicitly reports that delivery failed before handling.

If the renderer process exits before acknowledging a dispatched event, the implementation SHOULD retain or reconstruct the pending event so it can be retried after a new renderer becomes ready.

### 7.5 Renderer Protocol Navigation

The renderer protocol navigator MUST:

- Resolve repository targets against the current configured/opened repository set.
- Resolve ticket targets against the target repository before presenting the ticket view.
- Navigate only after the target has either resolved successfully or a deterministic fallback route has been selected.
- Focus or reveal the target view when navigation succeeds.
- Avoid duplicate navigation if the current route already represents the target.

Repository targets MUST route to the repository issue list for that repository. Ticket targets MUST route to the ticket detail view for that repository.

If required repository data has not loaded yet, the renderer SHOULD wait for the normal bootstrap/repository query path to settle. It MUST NOT wait indefinitely without visible progress or a visible failure state.

## 8. Failure Model

A conforming implementation MUST classify failures into these categories:

| Category | Examples | Required behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `malformed_url` | invalid URL, wrong scheme, empty segment, query/fragment in v1 | Do not navigate; show invalid-link feedback; log structured reason. |
| `unsupported_target` | room, board, memory, or future target not implemented | Do not guess; show unsupported-target feedback. |
| `repository_missing` | repository id is not registered or not available | Route to a safe workspace fallback and show repository-missing feedback. |
| `ticket_missing` | ticket id is absent, deleted, or not materialized | Route to the repository issue list or not-found state and show ticket-missing feedback. |
| `unauthorized` | future multi-account or permission failure | Do not expose hidden data; show access-denied feedback. |
| `renderer_not_ready` | cold start before router mount | Queue until ready; do not drop silently. |
| `delivery_failed` | renderer crashed or IPC send failed | Retry when safe or surface an operator-visible log. |

Fallback UI MAY use an existing notification, route error screen, empty state, or issue/repository not-found screen, but it MUST be visible to the user and testable.

## 9. Security and Safety Requirements

- `cycle://` input MUST be treated as untrusted even when it originates inside Cycle-rendered markdown.
- Protocol parsing MUST NOT execute code, open files, spawn commands, perform network requests, or invoke shell APIs.
- Parsed identifiers MUST be treated as data only.
- The existing renderer-to-main `openExternal` behavior MUST remain restricted to explicitly supported external protocols such as `http:`, `https:`, and `mailto:`.
- The implementation MUST NOT add `cycle:` to the generic external URL allowlist.
- Logs MUST NOT include query strings, fragments, credentials, or secrets. Because v1 rejects query strings and fragments, structured logs SHOULD record target kind, repository id, ticket id, and failure reason only where those fields are already visible to the user.
- Unknown target kinds MUST be rejected closed.

## 10. Observability Requirements

The implementation SHOULD emit structured debug or telemetry events using existing desktop logging patterns where available.

At minimum, the following transitions SHOULD be observable in development logs or equivalent diagnostics:

- Protocol registration attempted, succeeded, failed, or skipped.
- External protocol URL received.
- Protocol URL parsed or rejected.
- Protocol event queued.
- Renderer readiness received.
- Protocol event dispatched.
- Renderer navigation succeeded.
- Renderer navigation failed with a normalized failure category.

Logs SHOULD include `eventId`, `source`, `targetKind`, `repositoryId`, `ticketId`, `failureCategory`, and `reason` when available and safe.

## 11. Reference Workflows

### 11.1 Internal Click

1. User activates a rendered `cycle://` link inside Cycle.
2. Renderer intercepts the click before external navigation.
3. Renderer parses the URL with the shared parser.
4. Renderer resolves the target.
5. Renderer navigates to the mapped workspace route or displays a user-visible fallback.

### 11.2 External Cold Start

1. Operating system launches Cycle with a `cycle://` URL.
2. Main process captures the raw URL before or during startup.
3. Main process parses the URL and queues a protocol event.
4. Main process creates the main window and starts normal bootstrap.
5. Renderer mounts and signals protocol readiness.
6. Main process dispatches the queued event exactly once.
7. Renderer resolves and navigates to the target or displays a fallback.

### 11.3 External Already-Running App

1. Operating system sends a `cycle://` URL to Cycle while an instance is already running.
2. The existing instance receives the URL through the platform-specific second-instance path.
3. Main process focuses or restores the main window.
4. Main process queues or dispatches the protocol event depending on renderer readiness.
5. Renderer handles the event exactly once.

## 12. Validation Matrix

A conforming implementation MUST include automated coverage for:

- Valid parsing of `cycle://repository/<repositoryId>`.
- Valid parsing of `cycle://repository/<repositoryId>/tickets/<ticketId>`.
- Canonical serialization of repository and ticket targets.
- Percent-encoded repository and ticket identifiers.
- Rejection of wrong schemes, empty segments, query strings, fragments, credentials, ports, unsupported targets, and unsafe decoded identifiers.
- Mapping parsed targets to existing workspace route locations.
- Internal click handling that prevents external navigation for `cycle://` links.
- Preservation of existing `http:`, `https:`, and `mailto:` external-link behavior.
- Main-process queuing before renderer readiness.
- FIFO dispatch after renderer readiness.
- Duplicate prevention for a single external protocol event.
- Existing-instance handling for second-instance protocol opens where testable.
- User-visible fallback behavior for malformed, missing repository, and missing ticket cases.

Manual QA MUST cover packaged-app protocol registration for macOS, Windows, and Linux where release packaging supports those platforms. If a platform is not supported by current packaging, the QA note MUST state that explicitly.

## 13. Definition of Done

- Repository and ticket `cycle://` URI forms are specified, parsed, serialized, and tested.
- Internal Cycle-rendered links route in-app and never pass through generic external URL opening.
- External OS-level `cycle://` links open or focus Cycle Desktop on supported platforms.
- Cold-start links are queued until renderer readiness and routed exactly once.
- Already-running app links focus the existing app instance and route exactly once.
- Invalid, unsupported, missing, unauthorized, and deleted targets have visible fallback behavior.
- Security tests confirm `cycle:` is not allowed through generic `openExternal` IPC.
- Route mapping uses the existing workspace route model.
- Platform registration and manual QA notes are documented.

## 14. Open Questions

- Should v1 support only repositories and tickets, or should saved views, rooms, boards, and project memory references be specified before implementation starts?
- Should protocol links include workspace/account identifiers to disambiguate references across multiple signed-in contexts or future hosted workspaces?
- Should web builds intercept `cycle://` links for in-app routing, or should this behavior remain desktop-only?
- What exact user-facing component should own unresolved-link feedback: notification, route error screen, empty state, or a dedicated deep-link resolution screen?
